site.title: Rare Earth Atlas
site.subtitle: A bilingual museum / tech-magazine project on the 17 strategic elements behind magnets, displays, vehicles, medicine and industrial systems.

hero.title: Rare earths explained without the usual fog machine
hero.lead: A modern bilingual atlas with real photography, clear explanations and curated articles about the materials hidden inside magnets, displays, electric vehicles, medical imaging and industrial systems.

sections.architectureLead: From ore and refining to components, devices and strategic systems.
sections.whyThisMattersLead: Rare earths are not just chemistry trivia. They sit inside modern supply chains, electronics, energy systems and medical imaging.
sections.editorialDeskLead: Think museum wall text with less dust. A short editorial path from material to device and from device to geopolitics.
sections.mediaLedgerLead: Photography of materials, ores, devices and industrial stages that makes the theory easier to see in practice.
sections.curatedArchiveLead: Selected visuals that connect samples, devices, vehicles and industrial settings into one editorial thread.

cards.discoverLead: Starting points for elements, applications and imagery.

filters.searchPlaceholder: Search by title, symbol or use

matterCards.title: Magnets and motion
matterCards.text: Neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium and samarium make compact motors, speakers and actuators possible.

matterCards.title: Displays and optics
matterCards.text: Europium, terbium, yttrium and lanthanum appear in phosphors, optical glass and detector materials.

matterCards.title: Medicine and imaging
matterCards.text: Gadolinium and lutetium show that rare earths are also deeply woven into diagnostic technologies.

architecturePoints: Rare earths rarely matter as raw rock alone. Their value rises when they become purified oxides, alloys and functional components.
The same element can appear across more than one chain, from sound and optics to medical imaging and energy systems.
The bottleneck is not only mining. It is often separation, refining and the manufacture of magnets or specialty crystals.
Very small volumes can carry huge importance when they end up inside critical devices, sensors or precision systems.
That is why rare earths sit at the intersection of technology, industry, defense, medicine and geopolitics.
home.fieldGuideIntro: If you treat rare earths as a list of chemical names, you miss the interesting part. The practical way to understand them is to follow them from ore to processing, from materials to finished products.
home.fieldGuideOutro: That is the best way to use this atlas: start with an element, see where it is used, look at the linked object or machine, then move into the articles to understand the industrial and strategic weight behind it.

page.elementsLead: Filter the full family and inspect where each one is used.
page.applicationsLead: Applications from consumer tech to energy, medicine, automotive engineering and precision industry.
page.galleryLead: A symmetrical visual archive with cleaner crops, lightbox viewing and curated imagery spanning materials, devices, machines and industrial stages.
page.articlesLead: Longer-form texts and essays on materials, supply chains, technology and real-world uses.
page.sourcesLead: Official or high-value references to support the content and future expansion.

footer.copy: Rare Earth Atlas. A bilingual knowledge project about the 17 elements that support modern devices, energy systems and medical technology.

common.apiReadyNote: Keep the main point in view: the value of rare earths does not stop at the ore body. It grows through processing and through the components built from it.
